params.description: Horizontal picture graph appears showing Favorite Fruits data. Apples=6, Bananas=4, Oranges=5, Grapes=3. Key visible: each fruit symbol = 1 vote.
Data table appears alongside the graph, showing the same Favorite Fruits values.

steps.text: You made a graph with your Minis. Now let's read some other graphs. A graph isn't just a picture to look at. Every part gives us information to READ. Look at this Favorite Fruits graph.
Click on the category that got the MOST votes.

validator.description: Student clicked Apples — correct, 6 votes is the highest value

steps.text: Apples got the most. 6 people chose it. You read that from the graph.

elements.description: Horizontal picture graph showing Favorite Fruits. Apples row highlighted as the category with most votes.
Data table showing Favorite Fruits values.
Horizontal picture graph in reading mode. Favorite Fruits data. Apples=6, Bananas=4, Oranges=5, Grapes=3. click_category tool active.
Data table showing Favorite Fruits values alongside the graph.

steps.text: You found the most. Now find the opposite. Which fruit got the LEAST votes?
Which fruit got the LEAST votes?

validator.description: Student clicked Grapes — correct, 3 votes is the lowest value

steps.text: Grapes got the least with only 3 votes. You can compare categories by reading each row.

elements.description: Horizontal picture graph showing Favorite Fruits. Grapes row highlighted as the category with least votes.
Data table showing Favorite Fruits values.
Horizontal picture graph in reading mode. Favorite Fruits data. click_category tool active.
Data table showing Favorite Fruits values.

params.description: Vertical picture graph appears showing Animals at the Zoo data. Lions=5, Monkeys=7, Elephants=3, Penguins=6. Key visible: each animal icon = 1 animal.
Data table appears alongside the graph, showing the same Animals at the Zoo values.

steps.text: Every part of a graph has a job. Watch.
The TITLE tells us what the data is about: Animals at the Zoo.

params.description: Category labels highlight on the graph.

steps.text: The LABELS tell us what each row shows.
The KEY shows the SCALE. Here, each animal picture equals 1 animal. That's a scale of 1.
Now you try. Click on the part that tells us what each symbol means.
Click on the part that tells us what each symbol means.

validator.description: Student clicked the key — correct component

steps.text: That's the key. It shows the scale. Each symbol equals 1 animal.

elements.description: Vertical picture graph showing Animals at the Zoo. Key highlighted as the component that shows the scale.
Data table showing Animals at the Zoo values.
Vertical picture graph in reading mode. Animals at the Zoo data. Lions=5, Monkeys=7, Elephants=3, Penguins=6. click_component tool active targeting key.
Data table showing Animals at the Zoo values.

steps.text: Here's a different picture graph. This one goes up and down. We call that VERTICAL. Same idea. Use the key to read it.
How many monkeys are at the zoo?
7 monkeys. Each symbol equals 1, so you count 7 symbols.

elements.description: Vertical picture graph showing Animals at the Zoo. Monkeys column showing 7 symbols.
Data table showing Animals at the Zoo values.
Vertical picture graph in reading mode. Animals at the Zoo data. multiple_choice tool active.
Data table showing Animals at the Zoo values.

steps.text: Let's read one more value from this graph. Which animal has FEWER than 4 at the zoo?
Which animal has FEWER than 4 at the zoo? Click on it.

validator.description: Student clicked Elephants — correct, 3 is fewer than 4

steps.text: Elephants. There are only 3, which is fewer than 4.

elements.description: Vertical picture graph showing Animals at the Zoo. Elephants column highlighted showing 3 symbols.
Data table showing Animals at the Zoo values.
Vertical picture graph in reading mode. Animals at the Zoo data. click_category tool active.
Data table showing Animals at the Zoo values.

steps.text: You're reading picture graphs like a pro. Now let's see another way to show data: bar graphs.

params.description: Picture graph transforms into a vertical bar graph with axis 0 to 8, same Animals at the Zoo data. Bars replace symbol columns.

steps.text: Same information, different format. Instead of counting symbols, we read where the bar ends.

elements.description: Vertical bar graph showing Animals at the Zoo data. Axis 0 to 8 by 1s. Lions=5, Monkeys=7, Elephants=3, Penguins=6.
Data table showing Animals at the Zoo values.

steps.text: In a bar graph, the HEIGHT of the bar tells us the amount. Look at the Monkeys bar. How many monkeys does the bar show? Click on the Monkeys bar and find where the bar ends.
Click on the bar for Monkeys and find where the bar ends.

params.description: Horizontal line draws from top of Monkeys bar perpendicular to vertical axis. Value 7 highlights on axis.

steps.text: 7 monkeys. Same as the picture graph. The bar goes up to 7.

elements.description: Vertical bar graph showing Animals at the Zoo. Monkeys bar highlighted with guideline to axis showing value 7.
Data table showing Animals at the Zoo values.
Vertical bar graph in reading mode. Animals at the Zoo data. click_category tool active.
Data table showing Animals at the Zoo values.

params.description: Vertical bar graph appears showing Books Read This Week data. Axis 0 to 8 by 1s. Maya=4, Aiden=6, Sofia=5, James=7.
Data table appears alongside the graph, showing the same Books Read This Week values.

steps.text: Here's a new bar graph. The scale is 1. Each mark on the side means 1 book. How many books did Sofia read? Select your answer.
How many books did Sofia read?

params.description: Horizontal line draws from top of Sofia's bar perpendicular to vertical axis. Value 5 highlights on axis.

steps.text: Sofia read 5 books. You read that from where her bar ends.

elements.description: Vertical bar graph showing Books Read This Week. Sofia's bar highlighted with guideline to axis showing value 5.
Data table showing Books Read This Week values.
Vertical bar graph in reading mode. Books Read This Week data. multiple_choice tool active.
Data table showing Books Read This Week values.

steps.text: Graphs make comparing easy. You can see which is most and least at a glance by the height of the bars. Who read the FEWEST books this week?
Who read the FEWEST books this week?

validator.description: Student clicked Maya — correct, 4 books is the lowest value

steps.text: Maya read the fewest books: 4 books. It's the shortest bar.

elements.description: Vertical bar graph showing Books Read This Week. Maya's bar highlighted as the shortest.
Data table showing Books Read This Week values.
Vertical bar graph in reading mode. Books Read This Week data. click_category tool active.
Data table showing Books Read This Week values.

params.description: Horizontal bar graph appears showing Favorite Colors data. Axis 0 to 10 by 1s labeled Number of Votes. Blue=8, Red=5, Green=4, Yellow=6.
Data table appears alongside the graph, showing the same Favorite Colors values.

steps.text: This bar graph goes sideways. We call that HORIZONTAL. The LENGTH of the bar tells us the amount.
Which color is the most popular? Click on the longest bar.

validator.description: Student clicked Blue — correct, 8 votes is the highest value

steps.text: Blue is the most popular. 8 students chose it. It has the longest bar.

elements.description: Horizontal bar graph showing Favorite Colors. Blue bar highlighted as the longest.
Data table showing Favorite Colors values.
Horizontal bar graph in reading mode. Favorite Colors data. click_category tool active.
Data table showing Favorite Colors values.

steps.text: Same graph, different question. Which color got the LEAST votes?
Which color got the LEAST votes?

validator.description: Student clicked Green — correct, 4 votes is the lowest value

steps.text: Green got the least. Only 4 votes. It's the shortest bar.

elements.description: Horizontal bar graph showing Favorite Colors. Green bar highlighted as the shortest.
Data table showing Favorite Colors values.
Horizontal bar graph in reading mode. Favorite Colors data. click_category tool active.
Data table showing Favorite Colors values.

steps.text: You found the most and the fewest. Now let's answer some different kinds of questions.

elements.description: Horizontal bar graph showing Favorite Colors. Data table no longer visible.

steps.text: How many MORE students chose Blue than Yellow?
This is a COMPARISON question. How many more means we COMPARE two amounts. We find the DIFFERENCE by subtracting.
Blue has 8. Yellow has 6. Since more students like Blue, subtract Blue minus Yellow. How many more students chose Blue than Yellow?
How many more students chose Blue than Yellow?
2 more students. You found the difference: 8 minus 6 equals 2.

elements.description: Horizontal bar graph showing Favorite Colors. Blue and Yellow bars highlighted showing the comparison.
Horizontal bar graph in reading mode. Favorite Colors data. Blue and Yellow bars highlighted. multiple_choice tool active.

steps.text: New question: How many FEWER students chose Red than Blue?
How many fewer is ALSO a comparison. We still find the difference.
How many fewer students chose Red than Blue?
3 fewer students. Blue has 8, Red has 5. The difference is 3.

elements.description: Horizontal bar graph showing Favorite Colors. Red and Blue bars highlighted showing the comparison.
Horizontal bar graph in reading mode. Favorite Colors data. Red and Blue bars highlighted. multiple_choice tool active.

steps.text: Here's a different kind of question: How many students chose Yellow OR Red IN ALL?
In all means we want the TOTAL. We ADD to find totals.
How many students chose Yellow or Red in all?
11 students in all. Yellow has 6, Red has 5. 6 plus 5 equals 11.

elements.description: Horizontal bar graph showing Favorite Colors. Yellow and Red bars highlighted showing the addition.
Horizontal bar graph in reading mode. Favorite Colors data. Yellow and Red bars highlighted. multiple_choice tool active.

steps.text: Sometimes you need TWO steps to answer a question. Let's try one together.
Question: How many MORE students chose Yellow or Red together compared to Green?
Step 1: Find how many chose Yellow or Red IN ALL. How many liked Yellow or Red in all?
How many liked Yellow or Red in all?
11 in all. Yellow has 6, Red has 5. 6 plus 5 equals 11.

params.description: Result from step 1 displayed: Yellow + Red = 11

elements.description: Horizontal bar graph showing Favorite Colors. Green bar now highlighted for comparison.
Result from step 1: Yellow + Red = 11
Horizontal bar graph in reading mode. Favorite Colors data. Yellow and Red bars highlighted. multiple_choice tool active.

steps.text: Step 2: Now compare that total to Green.
How many MORE is 11 than Green's 4?
7 more students like red or yellow compared to green. You used TWO steps: first you added to find a total, then you subtracted to compare.

elements.description: Horizontal bar graph showing Favorite Colors. Green bar highlighted.
Result from step 1: Yellow + Red = 11
Horizontal bar graph in reading mode. Favorite Colors data. Green bar highlighted. Result from step 1 displayed. multiple_choice tool active.
Result from step 1: Yellow + Red = 11

incorrects.description: Branch 2: Student selected only Dogs or only Fish (correct answers only, but incomplete)

steps.text: You picked a category that matters. The question asks about comparing two groups. Look for one more.

incorrects.description: Branch 3: Student selected only incorrect categories (Cats, Birds, or Lizards)

steps.text: Let's think about this together. The question asks how many fewer students have fish than dogs. Which two categories does that name?

incorrects.description: Branch 4: Student selected at least one correct category (Dogs or Fish) plus at least one incorrect category

steps.text: Here's a hint: you picked at least one category that fits. The question only compares fish and dogs, so you don't need the others.

incorrects.description: Student answered incorrectly. System models the correct answer.

params.description: Dogs and Fish columns pulse to highlight them as the two categories needed

steps.text: Let me show you how this works. The question asks how many fewer students have fish than dogs. That tells you exactly which two categories to look at: Dogs and Fish. The others don't matter for this question.
Now solve it. Remember: how many fewer means compare or find the difference. How many fewer students have fish than dogs?
How many fewer students have fish than dogs?
3 fewer students. 7 students have dogs and 4 students have Fish. The difference is 3.

elements.description: Vertical picture graph showing Pets We Have. Dogs and Fish columns highlighted showing the comparison. Cats, Birds, Lizards dimmed.
Vertical picture graph in reading mode. Pets We Have data. Dogs and Fish highlighted, others dimmed. multiple_choice tool active.

steps.text: Question: How many MORE students have Cats than Fish and Lizards combined?
First, find how many students have Fish OR Lizards at home.
How many have Fish or Lizards combined?
5 in all. 4 have Fish. 1 has Lizards. 4 plus 1 equals 5.

params.description: Result from step 1 displayed: Fish + Lizards = 5

elements.description: Vertical picture graph showing Pets We Have. Cats column now highlighted for comparison.
Result from step 1: Fish + Lizards = 5
Vertical picture graph in reading mode. Pets We Have data. multiple_choice tool active.

steps.text: Next, how many MORE students have Cats compared to Fish and Lizards together?
How many MORE have Cats than the total of Fish and Lizards?
4 more students have cats than fish or lizards at home. You used TWO steps to answer questions about the pets students have at home.

elements.description: Vertical picture graph showing Pets We Have. Cats column highlighted.
Result from step 1: Fish + Lizards = 5
Vertical picture graph in reading mode. Pets We Have data. Cats highlighted. Result from step 1 displayed. multiple_choice tool active.
Result from step 1: Fish + Lizards = 5

steps.text: You can read picture graphs and bar graphs. You know how many more and how many fewer mean subtract to find the difference. And in all means add to find the total. Let's see how you can apply what you learned.

elements.description: Vertical picture graph showing Pets We Have data.
Result from step 1: Fish + Lizards = 5
